feed.title: SAP Community - SD (Sales and Distribution)

entry.title: Flexible Item Category Determination in SAP Cloud Public - Custom Adaptions
entry.content:
Why Flexible Item Category Determination Matters!

As Sales Configuration Experts, we all know that the Item categories control how sales document items behave and thus meet different demands in the business processes.

However, the limitations of the Sales Public Cloud prevent the creation of Z-Sales Document Types. As a result, we are unable to control the determination of the appropriate item category that will control the item behavior in the sales documents. Consequently, most users are required to make manual changes when processing these documents.

Flexible Determination

Using the Flexible Item Category Determination, The system can evaluate specific business conditions and propose an item category according to your business rules instead of the Default Item Category assignment.

Example:

Your client needs to manage the behavior of business processes by selecting the appropriate Order Reason. For a certain type of orders, a down payment is required for all products. Therefore, the client will select "Down Payment" from the dropdown list. Once this selection is made, the system can propose the CBAO item category when specific business conditions outlined in a decision table are met, allowing the billing plan to be automatically enabled.

Define the following Business Rule to Your Decision Table.

 

Sales Document Type Item Category Group Item Category Usage Item Category of Higher-Level Item SD Order Reason Item Category

 

= OR= NORM<empty><empty>= 01 (Down Payment)= CBAO

Note: If the user doesn't select any order reason or any available alternatives, the system finds no logic in your decision table. The system proposes the default item category TAN.

Step-by-Step Guide to Configuration

Step 1: Create PRFplus Objects

you can create Business Rule Framework Plus (BRFplus) applications, functions, and expressions from templates.

You perform this in the configuration activity Create BRFplus Objects for Sales Document Categories.

  • Custom Adaption: ITEM_CATEGORY_DETERMINATITEION

 

  • Select Document Category: C (Orders) And Press Enter

     

A BRFplus application template is provided. The application template includes a function template and an expression template.

 

  • Press The Copy Button to Enter the PRFplus Object (PRFplus Application)  

     

     

  • Save Your Configuration
  • Navigate Simplified BRFplus Workbench app and Configure your Business Rules.

     

  • In the following Application you can define the new Business Rules to your Decision Table.
    • Find your application in the repository.
    • Expand the application node, the expression node, and then the decision table node.
    • Choose the decision table to display it.
    • Click Edit

 

 

 

  • Now you can add a row to your decision table and add values in cells as shown below.

     

  • You can now check, save and activate Your Business Rules.
     
  • Recommendation

    To prevent any inconsistencies between your business rules and the existing item category assignments in the Assign Item Categories configuration activity, choose values for the following conditions and the result by using the search help:

    • Sales document type
    • Item category group
    • Item category of higher-level item
    • Item usage
    • Item category

     Results

    The implementation is complete for the sales document category to which the activated BRFplus application, function, and expression are assigned. The flexible item category determination is enabled for sales documents that belong to the sales document category. 

Testing the Configuration

  1. Create Standard Sales Order And Choose the Order Reason defined in your Business Rule.

 

Thanks!

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Functionality for EDI Processing: Inbound Order Creation by API vs. IDOC (Part 1)
entry.content:
In my previous post, I described how to select an API for creating orders based on EDI data and how to test a payload using SE80. This article focuses on a key aspect of EDI integration: mapping external partners to internal partners and determining the sales area. The mapping of external material numbers to internal ones will be covered in the next part. My goal is to compare whether the API supports the features available for IDocs, identify any new opportunities, and highlight deviations that require consideration.

The purpose of this analysis is to evaluate whether the selected API can replace IDoc-based processing. To achieve this, I examine the logic that aligns closely with classical IDocs. While the API may offer extended functionality, such cases are beyond the scope of this article.

As mentioned in my previous post, the appropriate API for inbound order creation is ORDERREQUEST_IN: Sales Order/Customer Return - Create, Update, Cancel (B2B). This API is compared with the IDoc ORDERS processing, which uses process code ORDE and function module IDOC_INPUT_ORDERS.

TLDR: Main Comparison Results

The B2B API supports most of the IDoc logic in the described areas while introducing several useful new features:

  • The B2B API ORDERREQUEST_IN is not a wrapper for IDoc processing but a separate implementation.
  • Partner determination uses EDPAR, similar to IDoc processing.
  • New feature: Partner determination by GLN, based on partner master data.
  • Order type and sales area determination use EDSDC. Simplified logic is recommended, though legacy logic is also supported.
  • Material determination employs similar logic for vendor material and customer material info records.
  • New feature: Material and UoM determination by EAN/GTIN, based on material master data.
  • Legacy logic for EAN/GTIN based on VB12 is not supported, and additional cross-checks have been introduced in the determination logic.

For a detailed explanation of partner and sales area determination, refer to the following sections. This article is extensive, so the detailed description of material determination is published in the second part.

 

API ORDERREQUEST_IN: Technical Details

I could not find any clear and detailed documentation of the business logic for this API. Therefore, most of the information was derived through testing and analyzing the code. From a technical perspective, the ORDERREQUEST_IN API is not a wrapper for IDoc-related code; it is implemented as separate logic.

My investigation focused on the methods within the LOG_EDI_SD_IMPL_ORDER package. The logic is distributed across multiple classes, and in some cases, the comments within the methods provide valuable insights. For example, as shown below, the MAP_PRODUCT method in the CL_EDI_SD_ORDR_MAPPING class contains a sufficient description of its logic.

MAP_PRODUCT: material mapping logic for EDI in SD.

Partner Determination Logic

For illustration, I will use the same organizational model example as in the article about Partner Determination for Inbound Orders .

Your company is a supplier, and you have the following customer:

ACME Group is a large multi-division company operating across various markets. They leverage economies of scale by using a group entity to manage their EDI operations. In your market, they are represented by the legal entity ACME Corp, and you ship goods to their distribution centre, ACME DC.

ACME sends sales orders through their EDI provider, which converts the messages and forwards them to your EDI provider. Your provider then translates these messages into API requests for processing in your SAP system. As it is traditional for Europe, ACME uses GLNs (Global Location Numbers) for communication and business parties. Additional details about their GLNs are as follows:

Master Records in the Supplier's SAP ERP

  • ACME Group: GLN 7089900000009 - 246
  • ACME Corp: GLN 7089900000016 - 211
  • ACME DC: GLN 7089900000023 - 212

EDI flow: buyer to seller via providers

To create a sales order in the SAP system, external partners must be mapped to SAP master data as follows:

  • Sender → SAP Group Customer
  • Buyer → SAP Sold-to Party
  • Delivery Point → SAP Ship-to Party
  • Supplier → SAP Sales Organization and Distribution Channel

Partner Determination: Sender

The sender partner is specified in the  <MessageHeader> section, outside of the Order segments. I consider it a communication partner, as opposed to business partners like Sold-to and Ship-to.

Example of XML structure:

 

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
<MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2020-04-10T02:53:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
    <InternalID>246</InternalID>
    <StandardID>7089900000009</StandardID>
  </SenderParty>
</MessageHeader>

 

 

Based on the XML structure, two types of IDs can be used for sender determination:

  • InternalID
  • StandardID

The InternalID is the preferred value and will be processed regardless of the presence of the StandardID. Technically, it can be any dummy value, which will be visible in AIF monitoring. As mentioned in my previous article, any value is acceptable if business partners are provided as internal SAP numbers. However, this value is critical for EDPAR determination. In the EDPAR table, there is a validation to ensure that the partner number exists as a business partner. As a result, it is recommended to use a relevant partner number. In my example, I use customer 246, which represents an SAP group customer.

If the InternalID is not provided, the StandardID will be processed instead. According to comments in SAP program code, a GLN number is expected here (not a DUNS number, which would be useful for the US market).

SAP code includes the following comment:

 

 

*---> Only 1 standard ID should be sent (GLN) - DUNS is not supported
*---> If there is one, we assume it is a GLN

 

 

Partner determination is based on the GLN from customer master data. If a unique partner exists with the provided GLN, the corresponding SAP partner number will be determined and processed further. Otherwise, processing will fail.

In comparison, for IDOC processing, sender determination requires the setup of partner profiles using transaction code WE20 (Partner Profiles). These can be configured as either KU (Customer) or LS (Logical System), with different logic applied in the associated mappings. However, in the case of IDOC, an intermediate entity like the WE20 Partner Profile is not required.

Sender Partner Error Handling

Any value is applicable for InternalID, but it must match the EDPAR settings if these are in use. If the internal sender ID is incorrect and does not match the EDPAR settings, an error will occur during business partner determination. Business partner determination, which operates based on SAP internal numbers or the GLN from master data, is not affected by the sender ID.

If StandardID is used and the sender partner cannot be determined from the provided GLN (e.g., if it is missing or not unique), the error message is self-explanatory but does not specify the partner type in question.

GLN error: Unable to uniquely determine business partner in SAP EDI.

 

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global" xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:...">
  <standard>
    <faultText> Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
    <faultDetail>
      <severity>error</severity>
      <text>Business partner cannot be uniquely determined by GLN 7089900000099</text>
      <id>013(EDI_SD)</id>
    </faultDetail>
  </standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>

 

 

Partner Determination: Business Partners

Business partner information is stored in the Order/Party segment, and the basic determination logic is described in the code as follows:

 

 

*---> There are 3 possible ways to determine the party internal ID:
*1) If the supplier Party ID is sent, it is taken as the internal party ID 
*   - If incorrect → error
*2) If the buyer Party ID is sent, it is used to determine the internal party *ID 
*   - If incorrect → error
*3) If the global location number (GLN) is sent, it is used to determine the *internal party ID 
*   - GLN is retrieved from the BP master record

 

 

Similar to IDOC processing, the internal SAP number is the first option, followed by the external ID, which is determined using the EDPAR table mapping. However, unlike IDOC processing, an additional GLN step is included, leveraging the partner master data. Based on my experience, it was the most desired option to use GLN from customer master data, without any mapping tables!

For IDOC processing, my recommendation is to use the GLN as an external number and map it to SAP numbers via the EDPAR table (transaction VOE4) or, if the GLN is not provided, use the external partner number. For this API logic I see three reasonable options to consider:

  • GLN from master data (GlobalLocationNumber field) if the master data quality is sufficient for unique determination.
  • GLN as an external number, with additional maintenance effort but ensuring robust and separate mapping. In this case, the GLN must be treated as the BuyerPartyID.
  • External number (GlobalLocationNumber) if the GLN is not provided.

According to the code, sequential determination is not supported. For example, if the BuyerPartyID is provided but determination fails, the logic does not attempt to use the GLN, even if it is available.

While mapping the BuyerPartyID to SAP numbers uses the same EDPAR table, SAP provides a dedicated section in SPRO for SOAP API-related settings:
SPRO > Sales and Distribution > Electronic Data Interchange > EDI Messages > Convert External to Internal Partner Numbers.
I do not see any functional difference between using this SPRO option and the VOE4 (Sales EDI Conversion) transaction.

IMG configuration: External-to-internal partner number mapping in SAP EDI.

Let’s explore Business Partner Determination using examples of Sold-to and Ship-to determination with my test payloads.

Business Partner Determination by external partner numbers

The Party segment contains the Party type qualifier (PartyType) and the External Partner ID (BuyerPartyID). The GLN (Global Location Number) may also be provided in the GlobalLocationNumber field; however, if the BuyerPartyID is provided, the GLN will always be ignored. The qualifiers SoldTo and ShipTo are self-explanatory.

Here is a part of a test payload example:

 

 

<Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_201124_3</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-07</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
    <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0001</BuyerPartyID>
    <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
    <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0002</BuyerPartyID>
    <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
</Order>

 

 

To process such payloads, the following EDPAR settings are required:

EDPAR table: Mapping external partners to internal numbers in SAP.

In this case, 246 is the sender number, provided directly in SenderParty > InternalID or determined by the GLN specified in SenderParty > StandardID. The logic functions similarly to the Customer as Sender logic used for IDOCs.

I am testing this using SE80, as explained in my previous article.

API test request: XML structure for EDI order creation in SAP.

Order request example:

 

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
 <MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2024-11-23T02:58:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
<InternalID>246</InternalID>
   <StandardID>7089900000009</StandardID>
  </SenderParty>
 </MessageHeader>
 <Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_211124_01</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-22</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0001</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0002</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="Supplier">
   <BuyerPartyID>WHOLESALER_ASA</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7088800010002</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Text Type="SalesNote">
   <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
   <TextElementText>Header Note 1 by API</TextElementText>
  </Text>
  <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <OrderItemText>Customer Order item text</OrderItemText>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521</BuyerProductID>
   </Product>
   <ExpectedNetPrice>
    <Amount currencyCode="NOK">11</Amount>
    <BaseQuantity unitCode="PCE">1</BaseQuantity>
   </ExpectedNetPrice>
   <ExpectedNetAmount currencyCode="NOK">110</ExpectedNetAmount>
   <Text Type="MaterialText">
    <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
    <TextElementText>Material Sales Text by API</TextElementText>
   </Text>
  </OrderItem>
 </Order>
</n0:OrderRequest>

 

 

 

 

 

 

The result of processing can be found in /AIF/ERR - Monitoring and Error Handling. The sender partner number (246) and the customer PO number are used as nodes in the tree structure.

EDI error log: Key messages for inbound sales order processing.

The sales order created by this request is:

Standard Order: Overview of sales order created via EDI.

Business Partner Determination by GLNs

In this second example, I will demonstrate how the determination logic works based on GLN numbers stored in the partner master record (General Data > Identification).

Business Partner: Identification with GLN and check digit in SAP.

In the payload example below, the Sender, Sold-to, and Ship-to are determined by their respective GLNs:

API request XML: EDI order details for testing in SAP.

Order request example, GLN partners

 

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
 <MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2024-11-22T03:58:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
   <StandardID>7089900000009</StandardID>
  </SenderParty>
 </MessageHeader>
 <Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_211124_01_GLN</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-22</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="Supplier">
   <BuyerPartyID>WHOLESALER_ASA</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7088800010002</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Text Type="SalesNote">
   <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
   <TextElementText>Header Note 1 by API</TextElementText>
  </Text>
  <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <OrderItemText>Customer Order item text</OrderItemText>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521</BuyerProductID>
   </Product>
   <ExpectedNetPrice>
    <Amount currencyCode="NOK">11</Amount>
    <BaseQuantity unitCode="PCE">1</BaseQuantity>
   </ExpectedNetPrice>
   <ExpectedNetAmount currencyCode="NOK">110</ExpectedNetAmount>
   <Text Type="MaterialText">
    <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
    <TextElementText>Material Sales Text by API</TextElementText>
   </Text>
  </OrderItem>
 </Order>
</n0:OrderRequest>

 

 

Created sales order:

Standard Order: Overview of sales order created via EDI (GLN based determination)

Business Partner Determination: Error Handling

If an error occurs during partner determination, the fault description includes an explanation of which step failed.

For example, if partner determination fails during the step based on BuyerPartyID, the response will be as follows:

Error: Sold-to party role not determined in SAP EDI response.

 

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global"                        xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:...">
    <standard>
        <faultText>Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
        <faultDetail>
            <severity>error</severity>
            <text>Business partner for role Sold-to party cannot be determined</text>
            <id>003(EDI_SD)</id>
        </faultDetail>
    </standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>

 

 

If only the GlobalLocationNumber is provided and the business partner cannot be determined from the given GLN (e.g., if it is missing or not unique), the error message is self-explanatory but does not specify the partner type in question.

Error response: GLN ambiguity prevents business partner determination in EDI.

 

 

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global" 
                         xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:...">
    <standard>
        <faultText>Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
        <faultDetail>
            <severity>error</severity>
            <text>Business partner cannot be uniquely determined by GLN 7089900000096</text>
            <id>013(EDI_SD)</id>
        </faultDetail>
    </standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>

 

 

Order Type and Sales Area Determination Logic

When creating a sales order in the SAP system, the order type and sales organization data must be determined. SAP developers aimed to implement reasonable logic while considering legacy logic previously used, such as for IDOC processing. Some insights and reflections on this can be found in the program code comments.

Although sales area data assignment uses the same EDSDC table, SAP provides a dedicated section in SPRO for SOAP API-related settings:
SPRO > Sales and Distribution > Electronic Data Interchange > EDI Messages > Assign Customer/Supplier to Sales Organization Data.

I do not see any significant differences between using the SPRO option and transaction VOE2 (Sales EDI Customer/Supplier).

IMG: Assign customer/supplier to sales organization data in SAP EDI.

Several options are provided for determination logic. Let’s review the following option:

Order Type and Sales area determination by BuyerPartyID of Supplier Party 

According to the code comments, the recommended approach is to assign the Sold-to SAP customer number and the Supplier Party BuyerPartyID to the required order type and sales area.

 

 

*---> In the new standard case the BuyerPartyID of the Supplier Party (LF) is used to determine the sales area and document type.
*     This is the prefered option from now on.

 

 

Payload example (Sold-to is determined by EDPAR table):

 

 

 

<Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_201124_3</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-07</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0001</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0002</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="Supplier">
   <BuyerPartyID>WHOLESALER_ASA</BuyerPartyID>
  </Party>
  <OrderItem>

 

 

 

  • The EDPAR table is used to map the BuyerPartyID to the SAP customer number. This record must be created for Sold-to determination.

EDPAR table: Sold-to party mapping from external to internal number.

  • The EDSDC table is used to assign the order type and sales area to the SAP customer number of the Sold-to party and the BuyerPartyID for the Supplier party.

EDSDC table: Customer-to-vendor assignment with sales organization details.

Order Type and Sales Area Determination by BuyerPartyID of Sold-to Party 

According to the code comments, this option is not recommended but is supported for compatibility purposes.

In this case, the Supplier Party section is not required. The order type and sales area are assigned to the Sold-to SAP customer number and the external number of the same Sold-to party.

Payload example: 

 

 

<Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_211124_2</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-11-07</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0001</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0002</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
    <OrderItem>

 

 

Required Settings:

  • The EDPAR table is used to map the BuyerPartyID to the SAP customer number. This record must be created for Sold-to determination.

EDPAR table: External-to-internal number mapping for sold-to party.

  • The EDSDC table is used to assign the order type and sales area to the SAP customer number of the Sold-to party and the BuyerPartyID for the same Sold-to party.

EDSDC table: Mapping customer to vendor with sales org and division details.

Order type and Sales area Determination: Error handling

If no record matching the inbound parameters is found, the following response is received:

Error: Sales area cannot be determined in SAP EDI response.

 

 

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global" 
                         xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:...">
    <standard>
        <faultText>Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
        <faultDetail>
            <severity>error</severity>
            <text>Sales area cannot be determined</text>
            <id>001(EDI_SD)</id>
        </faultDetail>
    </standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>

 

 

Acknowledgements and System Overview

Thanks to my colleagues at Capgemini and others who explored this topic with me across numerous projects. Also, thanks to Capgemini for providing me with a sandbox system, which I used for investigations, creating test examples, and preparing screenshots. The sandbox was SAP S/4HANA 2022 (S/4HANA On-Premise Release 2022 SP 01, February 2023 FPS).

entry.title: Enabling Seamless Logistics: Configuring S/4HANA/TM Integration
entry.content:
This blog outlines the steps to manually configure SAP's order-to-cash process and its integration with SAP Transportation Management.

To begin with, it is essential to set up a company's Organizational structure, which is the framework in which all business transactions can be processed. The term "enterprise structure" is sometimes used to describe an organization's structure. A company's enterprise structure is mapped to SAP applications using organizational units.

SAP system acts like a central hub for all your business information. The Client sits at the top, representing your entire company. Nested within, the Controlling Area acts as the command center for finances. Company Codes function like unique identifiers for each legal entity within the organization. Sales teams manage customer terms through the Sales Organization, where they can further categorize them by divisions (think product lines). Plants, the powerhouses of production, can be factories, warehouses, or service centers. Within each Plant, Storage Locations function like designated zones to keep materials organized. This structured approach ensures SAP data remains clear and accessible, just like a well-designed filing system.

Steps: Configuring Organizational Structure and TM Integration

Step 1: Define Company

Path: SPRO > IMG > Enterprise Structure > Definition > Financial Accounting > Define Company

Tr. Code: OX15

Step 2: Define Company Code

Path: SPRO > IMG > Enterprise Structure > Definition > Financial Accounting > Edit, copy Delete Check Company code.

Tr.Code: OX02

Tip: Copy from the existing company code and edit the details.

Step 3: Define, Copy, Delete, Check Plant

Path: SPRO -> Enterprise Structure -> Definition -> Logistics General -> Define, copy, delete and check plant

Tr.Code: OX10

Tip: Copy from the existing Plant and edit the details.

Step 4: Define, Copy, Delete, Check Sales Organization

Path: SPRO –> Implementation Guide -> Enterprise Structure –> Definition –> Sales and Distribution -> Define, copy, delete, check Sales organization

In this example, the sales organization is copied from the standard sales org.(0001)

Step 5: Maintain Storage Location

Path: SPRO > IMG > Enterprise Structure > Definition > Material management > Maintain Storage Location.

Tr.Code: OX092

Enter the plant name (in this case it is T010)

Select standard storage location 0001 and click the copy option on the top menu

Rename the copied storage location with the desired naming convention (i.e., TSL1 here). In the “Addresses of storage locations” tab user can enter the address of the created storage location.

Step 6: Maintain Purchasing Organization

Path: SPRO -> IMG –> Enterprise Structure -> Definition –> Materials Management -> Maintain Purchase Organization.

Tr.Code: OX08

Select standard purchasing organization 0001 and click the copy option on the top menu

Rename the copied purchasing organization with the desired naming convention (i.e., TP01 here).

Step 7: Define, Copy, Delete, Check Shipping Point

Shipping point: Plants may have multiple shipping points depending on their specific dispatch needs. Fragile materials, for example, may require manual handling, while heavy items might necessitate tools like forklifts or cranes. Multiple plants in close proximity can share a single shipping point.

Path: SPRO->Enterprise Structure->Definition->Logistics Execution->Define, copy, delete, check shipping point

Tr.Code: OVXD

Click the “copy, delete, check shipping point” option

Select Shipping Point 0001 (standard Plant) and copy as xxxx (TSP1 in this case)

Step 8: Assign Company Code to Company

Path: SPRO – IMG – Enterprise Structure – Assignment – Financial Accounting – Assign company code to company

Tr.Code: OX16

Select the created company code and assign the company in the last column

Step 9: Assign Company Code to Credit Control Area

Path: SPRO – IMG – Enterprise Structure – Assignment – Financial Accounting  – Assign company code to credit control area

Tr. Code: OB38

Choose the appropriate credit control area for the selected company code. If a specific business need necessitates a custom credit control area, create one and assign it to the company code. Otherwise, use the standard credit control area (0001).

Step 10: Assign Company Code to Controlling Area

Path: SPRO – IMG – Enterprise Structure – Assignment – Controlling  – Assign company code to controlling area

Tr. Code: OX19

Select the controlling area from the table (in this case 0001) and click the “Assignment of company code (s)”

Choose the "New Entries" option and input the desired company code. In the case of copying from the standard company code (0001), the system will automatically assign the newly created company code to the controlling area.

Step 11: Assign Plant to Company Code

Path:  SPRO > IMG > Enterprise Structure > Assignment > Logistics – General > Assign plant – company code.

Tr. Code: OX18

Select the standard company code and click the copy icon from the top menu bar

Rename the copied company code and plant with the created company code and plant (T010)

Step 12: Assign Sales Organization to Company Code

Path: SPRO > IMG > Enterprise Structure > Assignment > Sales & Distribution > Assign sales organization to company code.

Tr. Code: OVX3N

Select the defined sales organization (T010) and assign a company code to the respective sales organization.

Step 13: Assign Distribution Channel to Sales Organization

Path: SPRO > IMG > Enterprise Structure > Assignment > Sales & Distribution > Assign distribution channel to sales organization

Tr. Code: OVXKN

Click "New Entries" and enter distribution channel "01" for sales organization T010. Alternatively, you can also create and assign custom distribution channels based on your specific business needs.

Step 14: Assign Division to Sales Organization

Path: SPRO > IMG > Enterprise Structure > Assignment > Sales & Distribution > Assign division to sales organization

Tr. Code: OVXAN

Click "New Entries" and enter product division "01" for sales organization T010. It is also possible to create and assign custom product divisions based on specific business needs.

Step 15: Set up the Sales Area

Path: SPRO > IMG>Enterprise Structure > Assignment > Sales and Distribution > Set up sales area

Tr. Code: OVXGN

Click “New Entries” and maintain sales organization, distribution channel, and product division (T010,01 and 01)

Step 16: Assign Sales organization-distribution channel-plant

Path: SPRO > SAP IMG > Enterprise Structure > Assignment > Sales & Distribution > Assign sales organization – distribution channel – plant

Tr. Code: OVX6N

Click “New Entries” and maintain sales organization, distribution channel, and plant (T010,01 and T010)

Step 17: Assign Purchasing organization to the company code.

Path: SPRO > IMG > Enterprise Structure > Assignment > Material Management > Assign purchase organization to company code

Tr. Code: OX01

Select the defined purchasing organization (TPO1) and assign company code T010

Step 18: Assign Purchasing Organization to the Plant

Path: SPRO > IMG > Enterprise Structure > Assignment > Material Management > Assign Purchasing organization to plant

Tr. Code: OX17

Click “New Entries” and maintain the Purchasing organization and plant (TP01 and T010)

Step 19: Assign Shipping Point to Plant

Path: SPRO > IMG > Enterprise Structure > Assignment >Logistic Execution > Assign Shipping Point to Plant

Tr. Code: OVXC

Select a plant (in this case T010) and click the assign icon on the top menu to enter the shipping point (TSP1)

Step 20: Assign Shipping points according to Storage Location

Path: SPRO>IMG>Logistics Execution>Shipping>Basic Shipping Functions>Shipping point and Goods Receiving Point Determination>Setup storage-Location-Dependent Shipping Point Determination>Assign Shipping Points According to Storage Location

Click “New Entries” and maintain Shipping conditions, Loading group, plant, and defined storage location and assign it to a defined shipping point.

Step 21: Assign Shipping Points

Path: SPRO>IMG>Logistics Execution>Shipping>Basic Shipping Functions>Shipping Point and Goods Receiving Point Determination>Assign Shipping Points

Tr. Code: OVL2

Assign proposed shipping point against shipping condition, loading group, and plant (T010)

Step 22: Assign Picking Locations

Path: SPRO>IMG>Logistics Execution>Shipping>Picking>Determine Picking Location> Assign Picking Locations

Tr. Code: OVL3

Assign defined storage location (TSL1) against shipping point, plant, and storage conditions

Step 23: Assignment of Material Ledger Types to Valuation Area

Tr. Code: OMX3

Enter the provided material ledger type (e.g., standard type 9000 for valuation area T010) and save.

Step 24: Activation of Material Ledger

Tr. Code: OMX1

To activate the material ledger, check the material ledger account box and input '2' in the price determination field to use standard pricing.

Now set the Material Ledger as active (Productive)

Tr. Code: CKMSTART

Enter the plant, Exchange Rate Type: M, Uncheck Background Processing, and Execute

Execute again going back and this time unchecking the Test Run.

Material Ledger is now Productive for the Plant

Step 25: Assignment of Company Code to the Chart of Accounts

Tr. Code: OB62

Enter the chart of accounts specified by the Finance team. For this example, use 'Sample chart of accounts-INT'.

Step 26: Account Determination for Valuation Areas

Tr. Code: OMWD

Enter the valuation group code as 0001 (standard)

Step 27: Create a Material Master Record

Step 1: Maintain Material Type

Tr. Code: OMS2

Select the Material Type and click the Quantity/value updating tab.

Search for the valuation area(T010) and check the "Qty updating" and "Value updating" fields. Click Save and Exit.

Step 2: Create Material Master

Tr. Code: MM01, Set industry sector to 'Retail Industry' and material type to 'Finished Product'.

Select Views: Basic Data 1, 2; Sales Org. Data 1, 2; General/Plant Data; MRP 1; Storage 1; Accounting 1.
Enter Basic Data: Plant, Storage Location, Material Description, Unit of Measure, Material Group, Division, and Dimensions.
Set Sales Data: Availability Check (KP), Loading Group (0001), Transportation Group (optional).
Set MRP Data: Unit of Measure, Purchasing Org., MRP Type, Lot Sizing Procedure, MRP Controller.
Set Accounting Data: Unit of Measure, Valuation Class, Price Control, Standard Price.
Save Material Master.

Step 28: Create a Business Partner/Customer

Tr. Code: BP

Click Organization. Depending on the business requirement, the user can select Person/Group.

Select Title as a company from the drop-down list. Enter the company name, Search term, address, Company postal code, and Language. Click save and Business partner (Gen.) is generated.

Extend the Business partner (Gen.) role by selecting from the drop-down list to FLCU01 Customer role. Click the Sales and Distribution option from the top menu bar

In the sales area, enter sales org., distribution channel, and division.

In the orders tab, enter currency group, price group, and customer pricing procedure.

In the shipping tab, enter shipping condition (01 Standard)

Extend the Business partner (Gen.) role by selecting from the drop-down list to FLCU00 FI Customer role. Click the Company code from the top menu bar

Enter the company code and reconciliation account

Step 29: Create Sales Order

Tr. Code: VA01

Select Order Type, sales organization, distribution channel, and division and click continue

In the sales order header, enter sold to party, ship to party(customer), customer reference, and press enter

In the sales tab, enter the relevant details and press enter.

Save the Sales Order

Step 30: Assigning control key to the sales order (to integrate with SAP TM)

To integrate with SAP TM, assign a control key to the sales order. This key links the order to SAP TM. Check the TM Status tab in the sales order to monitor the integration status and view the document flow.

Step 1: Define control keys for document integration

Path: SPRO>IMG>Transportation Management>Intergration with other SAP Components>Logistics Integration>Define Control Keys for Document integration

Step 2: Define the Logistics Integration Profile

Path: SPRO>IMG>Transportation Management>Intergration with other SAP Components>Logistics Integration>Internal TM Component Integration>Define Logistics Integration Profile

Step 3: Define Transportation Relevance of Sales Documents

Path: SPRO>IMG>Transportation Management>Intergration with other SAP Components>Logistics Integration>Define Transportation-Relevance of Sales Documents

Step 31: Re-Create Sales Order

Create a new sales order and check its TM Status tab to monitor the integration with SAP TM and view the document flow.

 

The steps described in the blog cover setting up organizational structures, creating material and customer master data, and configuring sales orders. A key aspect is integrating with SAP TM by assigning control keys to sales orders. By following these steps, you can establish a foundational SAP environment to support your business operations.

Your feedback and suggestions for improvement are welcome. Please share your thoughts in the comments section below.

entry.title: Obsolete syntax: the dangers of using COMMON PART
entry.content:
Recently i stumbled across a strange issue, that took me quite a long time to understand. Working on a customer development in ABAP, i needed to show available quantity(ATP) per storage location and LE delivery and then update one single field(storage location) on some LE delivery documents. The approach i took was to re-use(call) a couple of standard function modules, that i spotted during debugging the VL03 processing. The execution flow seemed correct, but i was noticing in the debugger that global variables defined in includes were having unexpected values. Mostly it was the internal tables xlips and ylips that are declared in the include VBLPDATA

The issue that i experienced was that the tables were empty. In comparison, during the seemingly same call sequence done from the transaction VL03n, they had values.

After some serious head scratching,  i read the documentation... Of the funny BEGIN OF COMMON PART syntax. It says the following

 

Now if you are like me, you don't think about program GROUPS often, or you maybe not even know what that is. Thankfully, there is this great blog from Olga Dolinskaja - New ABAP Debugger Tipps and Tricks - SAP Community
The debugger feature

" Loaded Programs shows you two tabs. On the Loaded Programs tab you see the attributes of all loaded programs and their program groups. "

was exactly what i needed.

I searched for the include in question, and was able to see that it was loaded in a different program group.

In plain words, the function module where i had issues, was not the first one using this INCLUDE. With the help of the debugger, i was able to see the program group where the table had been loaded to, and this was actually a different function module.

At the end i had to encapsulate my logic in a NEW TASK, so that i was absolutely the common data was being loaded in the right program group. In my case,  CALL FUNCTION IN UPDATE TASK would also have been an alternative.

Conclusion: please never, ever use the COMMON part syntax(it is obsolete anyway). Troubleshooting quickly becomes very difficult. Also, maybe familiarize yourselves with the loaded programs tab in the debugger. The knowledge can be quite useful, in the long run.

entry.title: Analyzing and Troubleshooting Credit Checks in Sales Documents
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

My name is Rodrigo, and I am writing this blog to address unexpected credit check results many customers encounter in their sales documents. To assist with troubleshooting, I will outline the steps I follow to analyze credit checks.

In this blog, I will explain the steps and also provide an example of analysis of credit check for sales order 290 in an internal SAP test system. Before analyzing, I advise you make sure customizing is correct as per KBA 2788718.

 The following apply to this analysis:

  • The system is a S/4HANA 2021;
  • I am not using connection to other systems to fetch credit data;
  • Standard BADI_SD_CM is used;
  • In the example, only credit limit check (static check step) is analyzed as it is the most common in customers scenarios. For a review of all the check steps available, I suggest checking this blog;
  • Old CHECK_CM report is used in the screenshots. A new version is about to be released soon which will suppress some obsolete data from ECC and also add some new information.

 

Step 1 – Verify OVA8 and report CHECK_CM:

Initially, I check the credit data of the sales document with report CHECK_CM. I execute the report with the sales document number and check the following fields:

  • VBAK/LIKP-KNKLI (credit account)
  • VBAK/LIKP-KKBER (credit control area)
  • VBAK/LIKP-CTLPC (risk category)
  • TVAK-CMGRA (credit control group) or TVLK-CMGRL/CMGRW (delivery credit group and goods issue credit group) in case of deliveries;
  • TVAK-KLIMP (if credit check is active for the order type, this field’s value should be ‘D’): for deliveries, this field does not exist and we check if there is credit check based on OVA8 combination.

Other fields in the report can be relevant in other steps of analysis. Using the fields mentioned above, I verify whether the customer has configured the credit check correctly for the desired scenario—be it sales order processing, delivery processing, or goods issue posting.. I verify it in transaction OVA8 with the combination of VBAK/TVLK-KKBER, VBAK/LIKP-CTLPC and document credit group (TVAK-CMGRA OR TVLK-CMGRL/CMGRW). For example, the sales order 290 has credit check configured for the combination credit control area ZRSC,  risk category ZR and credit control group 01 as you check from my screenshot below:

The flag ‘SAP Credit MNgt’ means that credit check will be performed for the document and the flag ‘Status/Block’ means that credit check status (VBAK/LIKP-CMPS_CM and VBAK/LIKP-CMGST) will be saved for the document based on the credit check result.

 

Step 2: Verify when the order gets the incorrect credit status.

The next step is to check the change log. I verify when VBAK-CMGST was changed to the unexpected value. If the credit status is set incorrectly at order creation, use the order creation date and time and move to step 3.

Let’s analyze why order 290 was blocked on 13.12.2024. In the change log, we can see the exact time when the order got blocked:

 

Step 3 – Check credit check log and transaction UKM_LOGS_DISPLAY:

Next, I go to transaction UKM_LOGS_DISPLAY. There, I set flag ‘Credit Check’, enter the BP that is in VBAK-KNKLI and execute for the time where VBAK-CMGST changes as per the order change log or order creation time.

Below we can check the credit check log for the credit account of the order 290 on 13.12.2024 next to the time that the change log informs the credit status change:

*You could also check the credit check log from the DCD in case of blocked orders that would have one.

So for the order 290, the credit limit was exceeded and that is the reason why it got blocked. If the date of the credit block is recent and credit data for the customer did not change significantly, we can simulate a new credit check in UKM_BP and verify if the credit check is being performed as expected. The steps to do that can be verified in the screenshots below:

We can see that the simulation executed the credit check and the result was the same from the credit check log in UKM_LOGS_DISPLAY. So, in this example, the result is correct and order 290 got blocked by static limit check because credit exposure is indeed exceeded.

If, for example, the order got blocked a month ago due to credit limit exceeded, the credit exposure in the present is probably different than what it was a month ago. Simulating a credit check now is not helpful then.

If you check in UKM_LOGS_DISPLAY and observe an unexpected credit check result in a not so recent past due to credit limit exceeded, there is not much SAP support could do to help without a reproducible example. If the credit exposure is suspected to be incorrect in the database, we recommend monitoring the credit exposure update log as outlined in SAP note 3235822 and attempting to reproduce the inconsistency.

If it is believed that the credit exposure was calculated incorrectly for the document during credit check, it is needed to verify the credit price of the order. For that analysis, the data from report CHECK_CM can be helpful.

 

Step 4 – Credit price analysis

For order 290, the credit price of item 10 (the only item in the order) is 2 EUR because VBAP-CMPNT is ‘X’, VBAP-FKREL is ‘A’ (delivery related billing relevance), VBAP-CMPRE is 1 and VBAP-KBMENG is 2. Below is the screenshot of the relevant VBAP fields for credit price analysis as we can check from report CHECK_CM:

  • VBAP-CMPNT (credit active flag): determines if the item is credit active, if it is ‘X’, it is considered in the credit price;
  • VBAP-FKREL (billing relevance): since the item is delivery related billing relevant, credit price is calculated based on the confirmed quantities not yet delivered;
  • VBAP-CMPRE (credit price): this is the credit price for each unit of the item which is configured as per KBA 2708680;
  • VBAP-KBMENG (confirmed quantity): this is the total confirmed quantity of the item.

Since the item 10 in order 290 had no quantity delivered yet, the credit price will be 2 (confirmed quantity) x 1 (credit price) = 2 EUR.

If you have checked and still suspect that credit check is calculating the credit price incorrectly, then we should do some debugging. For more detailed steps on how to debug credit price, you can refer to this blog.

Further details on credit price calculation can also be found in KBA 2501945.

Below I provide some hints for sales order credit check analysis. I will use the same example of order 290. For delivery documents, the analysis would be similar and the credit check would be in FM SD_DELIVERY_CREDIT_CHECK.

 

Credit check debugging hints:

Credit check for sales orders happens in FM SD_ORDER_CREDIT_CHECK. After editing an order, in transaction VA02, and clicking on Save, the FM is called to update credit status.

When we get there, we can set a breakpoint as per screenshot below and verify if the credit check will be indeed performed.

If those variables mentioned in the screenshot above are blank, we will then execute the credit check. Inside the credit check, in method IF_EX_BADI_SD_CM~FSCM_CREDIT_CHECK_ORDER, the variable LT_SCHEDULE will contain the information about the credit price. The amount in LT_SCHEDULE are calculated in method IF_EX_BADI_SD_CM~FSCM_COMMITMENT_UPDATE_ORDER and I described in more details how it works in the blog Debugging steps of sales order credit exposure update analysis from SD perspective that I mentioned previously.

In the following screenshot, the amount is 200 but the actual value is 2. This is due to the field formatting in internal code.

The check steps of the check rule are executed in method CHECK_ACCOUNT (CL_UKM_CREDIT_CHECKER). In the example below, check step 010 (static check is being called):

Inside the check step in method IF_EX_UKM_CHECK_STEP~CHECK_STEP (CL_IM_UKM_CHECK_010), the system will verify if the commitments are higher than the credit limit:

After credit check failing, we can expect a pop up informing why and then the order will be set a credit block (VBAK-CMGST = B). A DCD should be created or updated then.

 

I hope this blog serves as a valuable resource for investigating credit check issues. You are welcome to post any questions/feedback you may have in the Comments section.

Best Regards,

Rodrigo

Digital Core Sales

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Functionality for EDI Processing: Inbound Order Creation by API vs. IDOC (Part 2)
entry.content:
This is the second part of a detailed comparison of the order creation process using APIs and IDocs. The first part focused on partner mappings. In this part, I will examine the mapping of external material numbers to internal ones. My objective is to compare the features supported by the API with those available for IDocs, identify new opportunities, and highlight deviations that require attention. The purpose of this analysis is to evaluate whether the selected API can effectively replace IDoc-based processing. For this, I will focus on the logic that aligns closely with classical IDocs. While the API may offer extended functionality, such cases are beyond the scope of this article.

As mentioned in one of my previous posts, the appropriate API for inbound order creation is ORDERREQUEST_IN: Sales Order/Customer Return - Create, Update, Cancel (B2B). This API is compared with the IDoc ORDERS processing, which uses process code ORDE and function module IDOC_INPUT_ORDERS.

TLDR: Main Comparison Results (Material Determination)

The B2B API supports most of the IDoc logic in the described areas while introducing several useful new features:

  • Material determination employs similar logic for vendor material and customer material info records.
  • New feature: Material and UoM determination by EAN/GTIN, based on material master data.
  • Legacy logic for EAN/GTIN based on VB12 is not supported, and additional cross-checks have been introduced in the determination logic.
  • Text Item might be created if CMIR is not recognized

Material Determination Logic

This topic can be divided into two parts: business logic and technical implementation. In my previous article about IDoc processing for Material Determination for Inbound Orders, I shared my thoughts on business logic, including X12 and EDIFACT examples, approaches relevant for "contracts" and "catalogues", and key challenges from a business perspective. Naturally, these challenges and ideas also apply to API processing. For more details, please refer to that article.

This article focuses on the technical aspect—specifically, how material determination logic works for APIs in comparison to IDocs.

For illustration, I will use the same test cases as those used for IDocs. Generally, customers use the following options as input data:

  • Customer Material Number (CMIR): Each customer has their own ERP system and unique material numbers, as well as any associated coding.
  • Vendor Material Number: A customer may use this number on the order, though it could sometimes be outdated, incorrect, or related to reference material.
  • EAN/UPC/GTIN: A barcode and number used internationally for product identification.

Material number mapping

The ORDERREQUEST_IN API request structure provides transparent fields for these options, as well as for unit of measure (UoM) information:

 

 

<OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <OrderItemText>Customer Order item text</OrderItemText>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
      <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
      <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
      <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521</BuyerProductID>
   </Product>
</OrderItem>

 

 

Processing logic is described in the program code. The MAP_PRODUCT method in the CL_EDI_SD_ORDR_MAPPING class outlines the logic as follows:

 

 

* Material determination logic:
* Priority 1: Supplier Product ID (material_no)
* Priority 2: EAN code            (international_article_no)
* Priority 3: Buyer Product ID    (customer_material_no)
*
* If EAN does not match material -> error in GOAL
* If EAN does not match customer material -> ignore customer material
* If material does not match customer material -> ignore customer material

 

 

T-code SE80, MAP_PRODUCT method:

Material Mapping Logic in the ABAP method MAP_PRODUCT

From this description, it is clear that this logic differs from IDoc material determination in at least two ways: the sequence of priorities and the additional EAN/GTIN control.

Another noteworthy feature is the use of EAN/GTIN data directly from the material master record during the determination process. This capability, long desired by users, is now available out of the box!

Comparison of API and IDoc Logic for Material Determination

Recap of IDoc Logic

When the SAP standard function module IDOC_INPUT_ORDERS is used to process inbound orders, the material determination sequence is as follows:

  1. Vendor Material Number (Qualf = 002):

    • If the value is not recognized, the IDoc fails, regardless of other qualifiers.

  2. CMIR (Qualf = 001):

    • If the value is incorrect, the system attempts to use the EAN. If the EAN is not provided, the IDoc fails.

  3. EAN (Qualf = 003):

    • If the value is incorrect, the IDoc fails.

API Logic Sequence

For API processing, the material determination sequence is:

  1. Vendor Material Number (<SupplierProductID>):

    • If the value matches an SAP material number or material substitution record, determination is successful.
    • If the value is not recognized, the message fails, regardless of other fields.
    • If <GlobalTradeItemNumber> is provided, an EAN check is performed. If the material or UoM does not match the EAN, the message fails.

  2. EAN/GTIN (<GlobalTradeItemNumber>):

    • If a unique material is found based on the EAN, determination is successful.
    • If the EAN is not found, the message fails.
    • If multiple materials are found by EAN and <BuyerProductID> (CMIR) is provided, CMIR determination is performed. Otherwise, the message fails.

  3. CMIR (<BuyerProductID>):

    • If the customer-material record is found, determination is successful.
    • If <GlobalTradeItemNumber> is provided, an EAN check is performed. If the material or UoM does not match the EAN, the message fails.
    • Regardless of whether <BuyerProductID> is used for determination, it is recorded as the customer material number in the relevant sales order item field.

Key Differences Between IDoc and API Logic

  1. Sequence Differences:

    • IDoc Sequence: Vendor Material → CMIR → EAN.
    • API Sequence: Vendor Material → EAN → CMIR.

  2. Interdependency:

    • IDoc logic is linear, with values not influencing one another.
    • API logic introduces EAN checks. A material determined by Vendor Material or CMIR must match the EAN/GTIN if provided.

  3. Data Source for EAN/GTIN Determination:

    • IDoc uses material substitution (t-code VB12).
    • API uses material master data (t-code MM02, table MEAN).

Similarities Between IDoc and API Logic

  1. Vendor Material:

    • Both use the SAP material number or material substitution (t-code VB12).

  2. CMIR Determination:

    • Both rely on the same CMIR mapping (t-code VD52, table KNMT).

Conclusion

If only CMIR and Vendor Material are used for determination, the API and IDoc logic function identically. However, if EAN is included in the material determination process, the master data and determination logic must be carefully reviewed and adjusted.

Unit of Measure Determination Logic

Unit of Measure (UoM) determination is a critical feature for certain industries. I previously elaborated on this topic in the context of EDI and IDocs in the article UoM Determination for Inbound Orders.

The API follows a similar approach but includes some important adjustments:

  1. Mapping of UoM:

    • If the UoM is provided in the message, it is mapped from the external "ISO" code to the internal SAP code (t-code CUNI, table T006), just as it is for IDocs.

  2. EAN-Based UoM Validation:

    • If both the UoM and an EAN are provided in the message, an additional control ensures that the item's UoM matches the UoM associated with the EAN.

  3. Default UoM Logic:

    • If the UoM is not provided:
      • For Vendor Material or CMIR determination, the default value from the material master is used.
      • For EAN determination, the UoM connected to the EAN is used.

From my perspective, UoM determination based on EAN is a valuable enhancement introduced with the API. However, its effectiveness relies heavily on the quality of both the customer's and supplier's master data.

Material Determination Test Examples

Let’s review test cases to illustrate both successful processing scenarios and potential errors.

Vendor Material, EAN and CMIR

Example: Item with Vendor Material, EAN, and CMIR

In the message snippet below, all three external identifiers are provided:

 

 

 <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <OrderItemText>Customer Order item text</OrderItemText>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521-0000</BuyerProductID>
   </Product>

 

 

In this case, Vendor Material 1521 must be recognized either as an SAP material or through the material substitution technique, and the EAN 8427324815219 must correspond to the determined material. While the CMIR ACME-1521-0000 is not used for material determination, it is recorded as the customer material number in the relevant sales order item.

Below is the complete request structure for this test case:

 

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
 <MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2020-04-10T02:53:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
   <InternalID>246</InternalID>
  </SenderParty>
 </MessageHeader>
 <Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_23122024-1</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-12-23</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0211</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0212</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="Supplier">
   <BuyerPartyID>WHOLESALER_ASA</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7088800010002</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Text Type="SalesNote">
   <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
   <TextElementText>Header note 1 by API</TextElementText>
  </Text>
  <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <OrderItemText>Customer Order item text</OrderItemText>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521-0000</BuyerProductID>
   </Product>
   <ExpectedNetPrice>
    <Amount currencyCode="NOK">11</Amount>
    <BaseQuantity unitCode="PCE">1</BaseQuantity>
   </ExpectedNetPrice>
   <ExpectedNetAmount currencyCode="NOK">110</ExpectedNetAmount>
   <Text Type="MaterialText">
    <TextElementLanguage>EN</TextElementLanguage>
    <TextElementText>Material text by API</TextElementText>
   </Text>
  </OrderItem>
 </Order>
</n0:OrderRequest>

 

 

The Sales Order created by this request :

Sales order 1576

Material Master Validation: The EAN in the message (8427324815219) matches the EAN in the material's master record (t-code MM03) for the specified unit of measure (PC).

Material master: EAN details

Vendor Material, EAN and CMIR – error handling

Example: Incorrect Vendor Material

In this scenario, the Supplier Product Number (X1521) provided cannot be recognized by the system:

 

 

<Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <SupplierProductID>X1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521-0000</BuyerProductID>
</Product>

 

 

Error message: EAN/UPC 8427324815219 is incompatible with material X1521 and unit PC

The actual reason for the error is that the Supplier Product Number (X1521) was not recognized as a valid SAP material or substitution. However, since an EAN (8427324815219) was provided in the message, the system performed an additional validation to check the EAN's compatibility with the material and unit of measure (PC). The error description reflects the result of this EAN check, even though the primary issue lies with the unrecognized material.
Below is the complete error response:

 

 

<n0:StandardMessageFault xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPGlobal/Global" xmlns:prx="urn:sap.com:proxy:SW1:/1SAI/TAS86C5ECFCD060648BFE9A:757">
<standard>
<faultText> Execution failed; see further messages for details</faultText>
<faultDetail>
<severity>error</severity>
<text>EAN/UPC 8427324815219 is incompatible with material X1521 and unit PC</text>
<id>084(SLS_LORD)</id>
</faultDetail>
<faultDetail>
<severity>information</severity>
<text>Check route for Ship-to party 0000000212 in document header</text>
<id>008(VPD)</id>
</faultDetail>
<faultDetail>
<severity>information</severity>
<text>Taxes have been re-determined</text>
<id>217(V1)</id>
</faultDetail>
</standard>
</n0:StandardMessageFault>

 

 

Example: Incorrect EAN

In this scenario, the EAN provided (8427324815202) cannot be recognized by the system:

 

 

<Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815202</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521-0000</BuyerProductID>
</Product>

 

 

Error Message: No material exists for EAN/UPC 8427324815202

Since an EAN (8427324815202) was included in the message, the system attempted to validate it against the material master. The error occurred because this EAN could not be found or matched to a valid SAP material.

This example highlights a potential risk when using EAN as additional data. Even though the Vendor Material (1521) is correct and could be processed successfully, the inclusion of an invalid or unrecognized EAN causes the entire message to fail.

Example: EAN doesn't match UoM

In this scenario, the EAN provided (8427324815219) does not match the unit of measure (UoM) specified in the message:

 

 

 <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PAK">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
 <Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521-0000</BuyerProductID>
 </Product>

 

 

Error Message: EAN/UPC 8427324815219 is incompatible with material 1521 and unit PAC

Since an EAN (8427324815219) was provided, the system recognized it as relevant for Material 1521 and the UoM PC (Piece). However, the UoM specified in the message item was PAK, which was mapped to PAC during processing using the CUNI-related technique. This mismatch triggered the error.

Vendor Material– error handling

Example: Incorrect Vendor Material

In this scenario, the Supplier Product Number (X1521) provided cannot be recognized by the system and the EAN wasn't provided.

 

 

<Product>
    <SupplierProductID>X1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521-0000</BuyerProductID>
</Product>

 

 

Error Message: Material X1521 is not defined for sales org.1000, distr.chan.10, language EN

Example: Incorrect Unit of Measure

In this scenario, the Unit of Measure (XXX) provided cannot be recognized by the system and the EAN wasn't provided.

 

 

<RequestedQuantity unitCode="XXX">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
<Product>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521-0000</BuyerProductID>
</Product>

 

 

Error Message: Sales unit XXX is not defined for item 000010

Item with EAN

Example: Item with EAN

When using an EAN, both the material and unit of measure (UoM) can be determined. In this test case, the EAN 8427324815226 is recognized by the system as uniquely relevant to Material 1521 and the UoM PAC.

Below is the complete request structure for this test case:

 

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
 <MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2020-04-10T02:53:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
   <InternalID>246</InternalID>
  </SenderParty>
 </MessageHeader>
 <Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_23122024-3</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-12-23</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0211</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000016</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <BuyerPartyID>ACME-0212</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7089900000023</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="Supplier">
   <BuyerPartyID>WHOLESALER_ASA</BuyerPartyID>
   <GlobalLocationNumber>7088800010002</GlobalLocationNumber>
  </Party>
  <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <OrderItemText>Customer Order item text</OrderItemText>
   <RequestedQuantity>10.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815226</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
   </Product>
  </OrderItem>
 </Order>
</n0:OrderRequest>

 

 

Material Master data: The EAN in the message (8427324815226) is relevant for material 1521 and UoM PAC.

Material master: EAN details

The Sales Order created from this request successfully uses the UoM PAC, determined automatically based on the provided EAN.

Sales order 1577

Item with EAN – error handling

Example: Incorrect EAN

In this case EAN was clearly spoiled and couldn't be recognized:

 

 

<Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>X8427324815226</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
</Product>

 

 

Error Message: No material exists for EAN/UPC X8427324815226

Example: EAN is not unique

In this case, the EAN provided for material determination (8427324815219) is assigned to multiple materials in the system.

 

 

<Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
</Product>

 

 

MEAN table: EAN 8427324815219 is associated with two SAP materials: 1521 and 2591.

MEAN for 8427324815219

Error Message:More than one material exists for EAN/UPC 8427324815219

Notice: if a correct CMIR record was provided also, a material could be determined, as shown below.

Item with EAN and CMIR

Example: EAN Is Unique, CMIR Ignored

If material determination succeeds using an EAN, the CMIR is ignored. In this test case, the EAN 8427324815202 is recognized by the system as uniquely relevant to Material 2591 with the UoM PAC.

Request example:

 

 

<RequestedQuantity unitCode="PAK">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
<Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815202</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521</BuyerProductID>
</Product>

 

 

The EAN 8427324815202 is associated in the master data with Material 2591 and the UoM PAC.

8427324815202

While the CMIR value ACME-1521 is valid for SAP Material 1521 (t-code VD52), it was not used during material determination because the EAN was sufficient to uniquely identify the material.

CMIR for ACME

Sales order 1578 was created with material 2591. Despite being ignored for material determination, the CMIR ACME-1521 was recorded as the Customer Material Number in the sales order item.

Sales Order 1578

Example: EAN Is Not Unique, CMIR considered

As previously shown, the EAN 8427324815219 is assigned to multiple materials in the system. However, if the message also contains a relevant CMIR, the material can be uniquely recognized.

 

 

 <RequestedQuantity>10.0</RequestedQuantity>
 <Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521</BuyerProductID>
 </Product>

 

 

MEAN table: the EAN 8427324815219 is associated with two SAP materials: 1521 and 2591.

MEAN for 8427324815219

The CMIR value ACME-1521 is valid for SAP Material 1521 (t-code VD52). CMIR for ACME

By combining the EAN and the CMIR value, the system was able to identify Material 1521 as the correct material.

Sales Order 1579

Example: EAN Is Not Unique, CMIR doesn't match EAN

CMIR records helps if it matches a material determined by EAN. Otherwise determination fails as if CMIR wasn't provided. In the example below CMIR in the message is relevant to SAP material 1531, while EAN 8427324815219 is relevant for 1521 and 2591.

 

 

 <RequestedQuantity>10.0</RequestedQuantity>
 <Product>
    <GlobalTradeItemNumber>8427324815219</GlobalTradeItemNumber>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1531</BuyerProductID>
 </Product>

 

 

As result error appears. Error Message:More than one material exists for EAN/UPC 8427324815219

Item with CMIR

Example: CMIR is recognised

A correct CMIR is enough for material determination.

 

 

 <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
 <Product>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521</BuyerProductID>
 </Product>

 

 

The CMIR value ACME-1521 is valid for SAP Material 1521 (t-code VD52). CMIR for ACME

Sales order 1580 created:

Sales Order 1580

Example: CMIR Is Not Recognized, TATX Item Is Created

In this case, CMIR ACME-1521-0000 is not recognized in the system.

 

 

 <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
 <Product>
    <BuyerProductID>ACME-1521-0000</BuyerProductID>
 </Product>

 

 

Since the CMIR value ACME-1521-0000 cannot be determined, the system does not assign an SAP material number. Instead of generating an error, the system creates a text item (item category TATX). Such item doesn't contain SAP material number and quantity. Sales order with TATX item

This approach avoids a processing error and delegates the issue to customer service for manual resolution.

Note: This case is another difference between IDoc and API logic:

  • API Logic: When CMIR mapping is missing, the system creates a text item (TATX) as a fallback, enabling continued processing.
  • IDoc Logic: If CMIR mapping is missing, processing fails entirely, resulting in a hard error and blocking the order.

Acknowledgements and System Overview

Thanks to my colleagues at Capgemini and others who explored this topic with me across numerous projects. Also, thanks to Capgemini for providing me with a sandbox system, which I used for investigations, creating test examples, and preparing screenshots. The sandbox was SAP S/4HANA 2022 (S/4HANA On-Premise Release 2022 SP 01, February 2023 FPS).

entry.title: Managing Returns with 3 PL and Warehouse operations
entry.content:
Typically, in industries that are based on a model of selling to customers based on their consumption follow a consignment model. The consignment model works on a principle that the customer does not have to purchase all the mix of products upfront, but can keep the products within their promises and only pay for the products that they actually consume. This typically is a requirement for products that may be used in medical procedures where the actual implanted product in a patient is based on what may be required at the time of the procedure.

This presents a requirement for such businesses to ensure that they have all possible combinations provided to the medical facility to support a procedure, invoice the customer for the products used and get back as soon as possible the products that are not required so that they can be used as a source of supply for other customer requirements.

This means a way to be able to quickly receive the products in the warehouse and disposition them so that they can be put back into the supply chain.

SAP's return processing works on the premise that there is a ready to process document in the system when the products are received at the warehouse. If a document is not available, there is a need to create a document first before a return can be processed. Also, SAP expects a different set of documents if a product being returned back is a normal sales return ( using a returns document ) as against a consignment pick up.

In an environment where customers are expected to frequently return back consigned products that are not used, it is a frequent challenge that the products returned back could have been consigned to different locations of a customer. As an example, a hospital chain can have multiple receiving locations, but a centralized location to manage all returns that have to be sent back to the vendors. Most of the products are serialized and carry a unique serial number from a traceability persepctive.

The challenge is that the 3 PL or a warehouse would get a return back, but they have to be able to determine the correct document against which to process the return OR need to have a document created quickly so that the return can be processed. In a high velocity environment, that can present a challenge as the customer service team is typically not within an earshot reach of the warehouse or 3 PL.

What you need is a solution that can allow the 3 PL or warehouse to record the products that have been received and then have an automation solution that would determine if there is an open return document in the system to process the return OR have a new document created so that the return received and processed in the 3 PL can quickly be also transacted in the system.

In the scope of automations that we have, there is very little automation that is done for reverse logistics and a solution framework that allows for a seamless transfer of receipt and records in the SAP system would reduce the need for a manual intervention in such high velocity reverse logistics environments.

entry.title: Sell from Subcontractor - With Shipment Processing Part 1
entry.content:
Business Background

The sell-from-subcontractor cycle is a strategic solution that can be employed by companies to address manufacturing capacity constraints while ensuring efficient fulfilment of customer demands. In this process, raw materials are sent to a subcontractor, who leverages their facilities and expertise to manufacture finished goods in accordance with the company’s specifications. These goods are then maintained in customer consignment stock under the supplier’s ownership, providing flexibility and readiness for customer orders. When a customer demand arises, the company strategically authorizes the subcontractor to ship the finished products directly to the customer, bypassing internal logistics and minimizing lead times. This streamlined approach not only alleviates manufacturing bottlenecks but also enhances operational efficiency, reduces costs, and ensures prompt delivery. Leveraging SAP functionalities, such as subcontracting purchase orders, consignment stock management, and delivery processes, this cycle provides end-to-end visibility and control, enabling companies to maintain high levels of customer satisfaction and operational excellence. Below is the value chain diagram of the scenario.

Table of Content

    1. Material Master
    2. Business Partner
    3. Supplier Info Record
    4. Create Sales Order Subcontractor Fill-up
    5. Create Purchase Order
    6. Create Outbound Delivery - Raw Materials
    7. Create Shipment & Shipment Completion (Goods Issue)
    8. Post Goods Movement - Place in Storage
    9. Post Goods Movement - Goods Receipt
    10. Create Outbound Delivery - Customer Consignment
    11. Create Supplier Invoice & Outgoing Payment
    12. Bonus
    13. Resources

Visit https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-for-industrial-manufacturing-blogs/sell-from-subcontractor-with-shipment-processing-part-2/ba-p/13976869  for the Configuration Guide.

Master Data

The master data section will focus on maintaining key elements to enable the sell-from-subcontractor process effectively. This includes updating the material master by adding valuation types to support split valuation and defining special procurement keys to support subcontracting processes. Furthermore, the creation of business partner records will be discussed, emphasizing the integration of supplier and customer roles to streamline interactions across procurement and sales processes. Additionally, the supplier info record setup will be covered, establishing a link between materials and subcontractors while detailing procurement-specific attributes such as pricing, lead times, and subcontracting parameters. These setups ensure accurate and integrated master data, providing a solid foundation and automation throughout the sell-from-subcontractor cycle.

1. Material Master

1. First, for the finished goods, go to MRP 2 view in the material master and maintain procurement type X. The procurement key indicates that this material can be used for inhouse production. Therefore, being able to use it in production process as well as being able to use it in subcontracting scenario. Add special procurement 30. Special procurement is used to get the subcontracting service cost in the sales order cost estimate.

2. In the same view, add stock determination group Z001 that is maintained and mentioned in Stock Determination configuration section. The stock determination group will be used in the automatic determination of the internal finished goods valuation type.

3. In MRP 4, activate the version indicator by choosing production version, as this needs to be activated to be used in BOM.

4. In Accounting 1 view, add the valuation category created in the Split Valuation configuration section.

5. Now, after saving Accounting 1 view, maintain the standard price for the internal and external valuation types. Re-enter the Accounting 1 view and add the internal manufacturing valuation type first then repeat for the external subcontracting valuation type.
